question: For each activity, which division of the nervous system is chiefly responsible? Classify each row.

rows.label: Integrating information and choosing a response inside the brain
Consciously lifting your arm to wave at a friend
Your heart speeding up automatically during a scare
Feeling and reporting a pinprick on your fingertip
Digesting a meal without any conscious control

hints: First ask: is this processing in the brain/cord, voluntary muscle/sensation, or automatic organ control?
Voluntary movement and conscious sensation are somatic; automatic organ functions are autonomic; integration is the CNS.
explanation: Brain integration is the CNS; voluntary movement and conscious sensation are somatic; automatic heart and gut control are autonomic. The key split is voluntary/conscious (somatic) versus involuntary (autonomic), both riding on the PNS.

question: Sort each bodily change by the autonomic branch driving it.

items.label: Pupils dilate as headlights swerve toward you
Salivating and digesting during a calm dinner
Airways widen and heart pounds before a sprint
Heart rate settling as you drift toward sleep

hints: Sympathetic mobilizes energy for a threat; parasympathetic conserves it and runs digestion.
Digestion ramping up and heart slowing are rest-and-digest; pounding heart and dilated pupils are fight-or-flight.
explanation: Dilated pupils and a pounding heart mobilize the body (sympathetic); active digestion and a slowing heart conserve it (parasympathetic). Digestion is the tell: it runs under parasympathetic control and halts under sympathetic.

question: A student describes the nervous system. Highlight the one incorrect statement.

tokens.text: The central nervous system is the brain and spinal cord.
The somatic nervous system controls voluntary skeletal muscle.
When the sympathetic branch is active, the parasympathetic branch is completely switched off.
The two autonomic branches are antagonistic, keeping the body in balance.
The autonomic system governs involuntary functions like heart rate and digestion.

hints: Four statements are correct; one treats the two branches as a single on/off switch.
Both branches are always active - what changes is the BALANCE, not one flipping fully off.
explanation: The error is t3. Both autonomic branches are active at all times; arousal is a SHIFT in their balance toward the sympathetic side, not the parasympathetic branch switching off. Treating them as an on/off switch is the named pitfall.

question: Place each feature where it belongs for the somatic versus the autonomic nervous system.

items.label: Splits into sympathetic and parasympathetic branches
Is part of the peripheral nervous system
Makes up the brain and spinal cord

hints: Voluntary muscle and conscious sensation are somatic; involuntary organs and the two branches are autonomic.
Both are peripheral (overlap); the brain and cord are the CENTRAL system, so that fits neither.
explanation: The somatic system runs voluntary muscle and conscious sensation; the autonomic runs involuntary organs and divides into two branches. Both are part of the PNS (overlap). The brain and cord are the CNS, so that item belongs to neither.

question: Predict which branch takes over.
segments: Once a threat has passed and the body needs to return to a calm, energy-conserving baseline, the
hints: Recovery to calm is the job of the branch that conserves energy and resumes digestion.
The sympathetic branch mobilizes for threat; its opposite restores the baseline.
explanation: The parasympathetic branch restores the calm, energy-conserving baseline after a threat, undoing the sympathetic arousal. The somatic and central systems are not the antagonistic partner that handles this return to rest.
